paragraphs.en: Welcome to Signals & Threads, in-depth conversations about every layer of the tech stack, from Jane Street, the quantitative trading firm. I'm your host, Ron Minsky. Today I'm talking with Andrey Mokhov. Andrey is a researcher who has worked on many build systems and has done an awful lot of thinking about what they can and can't do. We're going to talk about build systems past, present and future. I'm really excited about this one. Andrey, welcome.
paragraphs.zh: 欢迎收听 Signals & Threads，这是来自量化交易公司 Jane Street 的深度技术对话节目，涵盖技术栈的每一个层面。我是主持人 Ron Minsky。今天我的嘉宾是 Andrey Mokhov。Andrey 是一位研究员，曾参与多个构建系统的研究工作，并对构建系统的能力边界做了大量深入的思考。我们今天要聊的是构建系统的过去、现在与未来。我对这期节目非常期待。Andrey，欢迎你。

paragraphs.en: Hi, Ron. It's great to be on the podcast. So what is a build system?  Build systems automate the execution of various tasks of software developers. The first use case is of course compiling software, which is what build systems were originally designed for. You just give a compiler the source files and then it produces binary. But these days, build systems are used for a much wider range of tasks: testing, documentation generation, running static analysis tools. As the scope of software development has grown, the role of build systems has grown alongside it.
paragraphs.zh: 嗨，Ron，很高兴能参加这个播客。那么什么是构建系统呢？构建系统可以自动化执行软件开发者的各种任务。第一个使用场景当然是编译软件，这也是构建系统最初被设计出来的目的——你只需要把源文件交给编译器，它就会生成二进制文件。但如今，构建系统被用于更广泛的任务：测试、文档生成、运行静态分析工具等等。随着软件开发规模的扩大，构建系统所承担的角色也随之增长。

paragraphs.en: Beyond just using simple scripts, what's the next step up available to developers in terms of automation tools?
paragraphs.zh: 除了使用简单的脚本之外，开发者在自动化工具方面还有哪些进阶选择？

paragraphs.en: Well, before, I don't know, 1976 when Make was developed, there was nothing else and people were just writing scripts. And these scripts had one obvious limitation - they would just run everything from scratch every time. So you modify a single file in a project and you had to rebuild the whole world. And that's inefficient. And of course, as projects get bigger and bigger, this becomes more and more of an issue. And so that's kind of the most basic step forward that make provides.
paragraphs.zh: 嗯，在 Make 被开发出来之前——大概是 1976 年之前吧——也没有别的选择，大家就是写脚本。而这些脚本有一个很明显的局限：每次都会从头运行所有东西。所以你在项目里改了一个文件，就得重新构建整个世界，效率很低。当然，随着项目越来越大，这个问题也越来越突出。所以这就是 Make 所带来的最基础的进步。

paragraphs.en: Which on some level is kind of astonishing, just given how old Make is.
paragraphs.zh: 从某种角度来说，这真的挺令人惊叹的，毕竟 Make 已经这么老了。

paragraphs.en: I'm amazed at how successful Make was and still is.
paragraphs.zh: Make 的成功程度，过去和现在都让我感到惊讶。

paragraphs.en: What was the thing that made Make such a big step forward?
paragraphs.zh: 是什么让 Make 成为了如此重大的进步？

paragraphs.en: Well, it was easy to use, right? You just write a few rules and the format for specifying those rules is pretty simple. And it has a very simple and predictable conceptual model of what it does. So those are the reasons why it was so successful back in the day and still continues to be used. So today, lots of projects still use Make. Obviously, there are other build systems that are better in various ways, but Make continues to be used because of its simplicity.
paragraphs.zh: 嗯，它用起来很简单嘛。你只需要写几条规则，规则的格式也相当直观。而且它有一个非常简单、非常可预测的概念模型。这就是它当年大获成功、并且延续至今的原因。今天，还是有很多项目在使用 Make。当然，在某些方面已经有更好的构建系统了，但 Make 凭借它的简单性一直被沿用下来。

paragraphs.en: So, you talk about having a simple conceptual model, and what is that model on some level when you compare it to, say, a build script? Like what does Make provide beyond what a build script provides?
paragraphs.zh: 你提到了简单的概念模型，那么跟构建脚本相比，这个模型在本质上是什么？Make 相比构建脚本到底多提供了什么？

paragraphs.en: The main benefit of using Make compared to a script is that Make only reruns the tasks that need to be rerun given the changes that were made. So basically Make's key service is incrementality, where by incrementality I mean you only redo the work that needs to be redone. And the other service that's provided is parallelism. So if the different build tasks are independent of each other, then Make can run them in parallel.
paragraphs.zh: 相比脚本，使用 Make 的主要好处是：Make 只会重新运行那些因为改动而需要重新运行的任务。所以 Make 的核心价值在于增量构建——所谓增量，就是只重做需要重做的工作。另一个价值是并行性。如果不同的构建任务互相独立，Make 就可以并行运行它们。

paragraphs.en: Right. Service number one that's provided is incrementality. Small changes require small amounts of work. Service number two is parallelism, which is to say, if you have things that can be run independently, then make can figure out how to run them at the same time. And both of these things are provided by that declarative model where you say "this depends on this", right?
paragraphs.zh: 对。第一个核心功能是增量构建——小改动只需要少量工作。第二个是并行性——如果有些任务可以独立运行，Make 能自动找到并发执行的方式。而这两个功能都来自于那个声明式的模型，也就是你说「这个依赖于那个」，对吧？

paragraphs.en: That's right. Yeah.
paragraphs.zh: 对，没错。

paragraphs.en: And this is important to developers kind of for obvious reasons, which is to say a key thing you want to do as a developer is when you're staring at your build system, is to not stare at it. And so you want it to be fast. And the way to make it fast is to not redo things you've already done and to take advantage of parallelism where it's available.
paragraphs.zh: 这对开发者来说重要性显而易见——作为开发者，面对构建系统的时候，最想做的事情就是不用一直盯着它。所以你希望它足够快。而让它快的方式，就是不重复做已经做过的事，以及在可能的地方利用并行性。

paragraphs.en: Yeah. Absolutely. Like trying to maintain this parallelism by hand in a build script would be pretty tricky, and it's really nice to have that dependency graph being maintained automatically.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，完全同意。在构建脚本里手动维护并行性会相当麻烦，所以能自动维护依赖图真的很方便。

paragraphs.en: Okay. This all sounds like a very short story. We had this problem, how to build things. A nice system was invented in 1976 and here we are. And I take it that's not the full story.
paragraphs.zh: 好的。听起来这是个很短的故事：我们有个构建问题，1976 年发明了一个很好的系统，然后就这样了。但我猜这不是故事的全部。

paragraphs.en: There are few things that are missing in Make. One of them as I mentioned, the way Make determines the things that need to be rerun is by timestamps. So it will look at the last modification time of a file and see if it's newer than the dependency, and then it will determine whether or not it needs to be rebuilt. But this is a heuristic, right? It's not a perfect solution. Sometimes you modify a file, but the actual content change doesn't matter. For example, you might add a comment to a file and the output binary would be the same. But in that case Make would still try to rerun the relevant tasks.
paragraphs.zh: Make 有几个不足之处。其中一个就是我提到的：Make 判断哪些任务需要重新运行的方式是基于时间戳。它会查看文件的最后修改时间，看看是否比依赖项更新，然后决定是否需要重新构建。但这只是一种启发式方法，并不完美。有时候你修改了一个文件，但实际内容的变化并不重要——比如你只是在文件里加了一条注释，输出的二进制文件其实完全一样。但 Make 在这种情况下还是会尝试重新运行相关任务。

paragraphs.en: Right. Essentially the problem is that the heuristic that Make uses for figuring out what needs to be rebuilt is, in both senses, not quite right. It's both overly conservative and overly aggressive. Overly conservative in that it might recompile things that don't need to be recompiled. And overly aggressive in that if something happens to a timestamp by means other than an actual change to the underlying file — say you check things out from version control in a different order — it might miss things that need to be rebuilt.
paragraphs.zh: 对。本质上的问题是，Make 用来判断什么需要重新构建的启发式方法，在两个方向上都不太准确。一方面过于保守——可能会重新编译那些不需要重新编译的内容；另一方面又可能过于激进——如果时间戳因为非实际文件内容变化的原因发生了改变，比如你用不同的顺序从版本控制里检出文件，它可能反而会漏掉真正需要重新构建的内容。

paragraphs.en: That's right. It's overly conservative, at the same time I like it a lot because it basically just relies on this one simple piece of information in the filesystem which is the last modification time. So that's why it's easy to use. But it's definitely a heuristic and this leads to various problems in practice.
paragraphs.zh: 对。它过于保守，但同时我也非常喜欢它，因为它基本上只依赖文件系统里的一个简单信息——最后修改时间。正是因为这样，它才容易使用。但它确实是一种启发式方法，在实际使用中会带来各种各样的问题。

paragraphs.en: Right. I guess in addition Make's timestamp-based recomputation is, as you say, overly conservative. And another kind of correctness concern is that it doesn't track inputs well. So if you change something like a compiler flag, Make won't notice that and you can end up in an inconsistent state where your build outputs don't actually correspond to the source you have.
paragraphs.zh: 对。我觉得除了你说的基于时间戳的重新计算过于保守之外，还有另一种正确性问题：Make 对输入的追踪不够完善。所以如果你改了类似编译器标志这样的东西，Make 不会注意到，最终可能导致构建产出物与你的源代码不一致的不一致状态。

paragraphs.en: Yeah and it's fairly common for example, that various like backup software, for example, would mess with your timestamps, which would make Make rebuild things that don't need to be rebuilt. And sometimes people write workarounds for this.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，而且很常见的一个情况是，各种备份软件会篡改你的时间戳，导致 Make 去重新构建那些根本不需要重新构建的东西。有时候大家还会专门为此写一些变通方案。

paragraphs.en: Something goes wrong with Make, the answer to this is never to figure out what happened. It is always make clean. You just throw everything away and start again.
paragraphs.zh: Make 出了什么问题，解决方案从来都不是去搞清楚发生了什么，永远都是 make clean。把所有东西都扔掉，重新开始。

paragraphs.en: Make works very well for an individual developer, but one example where it starts to become too slow is when you're building a very large project, for example, GHC. GHC is the Haskell compiler that I helped to maintain. And one of the scalability limitations in Make is that Make builds this dependency graph at the beginning, before starting execution. As your project gets bigger and bigger, this step can take longer and longer. And for GHC, this was actually taking on the order of a minute, which is of course totally unacceptable for a build system.
paragraphs.zh: Make 对于个人开发者来说工作得很好，但在构建非常大型的项目时就开始变得太慢了，比如 GHC。GHC 是我参与维护的 Haskell 编译器。Make 的一个可扩展性限制是：它在开始执行之前，会先构建整个依赖图。随着项目越来越大，这个步骤会花费越来越多的时间。对于 GHC 来说，这一步实际上需要大概一分钟左右，这对于一个构建系统来说当然是完全无法接受的。

paragraphs.en: In some sense, you're pointing at a scalability limitation with Make, it's a pretty deep limitation: Make constructs the entirety of the dependency graph before starting the build. This means a few things. One, it means that it takes a long time if the overall build graph is large. Two, it means you can't do dynamic dependencies. Meaning if the output of one build step affects what the dependencies of a later build step are, you can't express that with Make.
paragraphs.zh: 从某种意义上说，你指出了 Make 的一个可扩展性限制，而且这是个相当根本的限制：Make 在开始构建之前会先构建完整的依赖图。这意味着几件事。第一，如果整个构建图很大，就会花费很长时间。第二，你无法使用动态依赖。也就是说，如果某个构建步骤的输出会影响后续构建步骤的依赖关系，你在 Make 里是无法表达这一点的。

paragraphs.en: Yeah, and this is something that I learned about five years ago when I started to work on Hadrian build system for GHC. Hadrian is a new build system for GHC in the sense that the old build system was Make-based, and Hadrian replaces that with a system based on Shake. When you have this problem with Make, needing to express the build as a dependency graph that you can compute at the start, it means you have to then use macros or other tricks to work around this limitation. And that leads to Makefiles that are very complex, and as a developer you just have to read through a massive set of Makefiles to understand what's going on.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，这是我大约五年前开始做 GHC 的 Hadrian 构建系统时体会到的。Hadrian 是 GHC 的一个新构建系统，原来的构建系统是基于 Make 的，Hadrian 用一个基于 Shake 的系统取而代之。当你遇到 Make 的这个问题——需要在开始时就把构建表达成一个可以预先计算的依赖图——这意味着你不得不用宏或者其他技巧来绕过这个限制。这就导致 Makefile 变得非常复杂，作为开发者你不得不读完一大堆 Makefile 才能弄清楚发生了什么。

paragraphs.en: Just to give us a sense, roughly how big is GHC?
paragraphs.zh: 给我们一个概念，GHC 大概有多大？

paragraphs.en: GHC, well, is I think is a million line of code. It's pretty big. The Makefiles themselves are pretty big. I think they were maybe like 12,000 lines of Makefiles or so.
paragraphs.zh: GHC 嘛，我觉得大概有一百万行代码吧。挺大的。Makefile 本身也相当大，我记得大概有一万两千行左右。

paragraphs.en: I think the problem you're pointing out here is essentially that Make isn't great from a composability standpoint. In that if you have a complicated project, Make doesn't really give you great tools for managing the overall complexity, you have to roll your own.
paragraphs.zh: 我觉得你指出的问题本质上是：从可组合性的角度来看，Make 并不出色。对于复杂的项目，Make 并没有提供很好的工具来管理整体复杂性，你只能自己想办法。

paragraphs.en: You mentioned generating macros and this is one other limitation of Make: Make fundamentally requires that you specify your build rules statically before any of the build steps are executed. But sometimes you don't know what your build rules are until you've done some computation. For example, you might not know all the source files until you've actually run something to discover them. This is sometimes called the dynamic dependencies problem.
paragraphs.zh: 你提到了生成宏，这也是 Make 的另一个限制：Make 从根本上要求你在任何构建步骤执行之前就静态地指定所有构建规则。但有时候你要等做了一些计算之后才知道构建规则是什么。比如，你可能要实际运行某个东西去发现源文件之后，才能知道所有的源文件有哪些。这有时候被称为动态依赖问题。

paragraphs.en: The project here is again Hadrian, which is a new build system for GHC. What actually is the status of Hadrian today?
paragraphs.zh: 我们再来说说 Hadrian，也就是 GHC 的新构建系统。Hadrian 现在的状态是怎样的？

paragraphs.en: It is functional. I'm happy to see that many people are using it, but the difficulty in switching from the Make-based build system to Hadrian is the sheer complexity of the existing Make build system. And when you work on migration like this, you kind of feel like an archaeologist. You're looking at these massive Makefiles and you have to sort out: what parts of the Makefile are essential for correctness and you have to port? And what parts are just dead code, hacks and workarounds that have accumulated over decades? And it's really hard to tell the difference.
paragraphs.zh: 它已经可以正常使用了。看到很多人在用它，我很高兴。但是从基于 Make 的构建系统迁移到 Hadrian 的难点，在于现有 Make 构建系统本身的复杂程度。做这种迁移工作的时候，你有点像在做考古。你面对这些庞大的 Makefile，必须分辨出：哪些部分是保证正确性所必须的，需要移植过去？哪些部分只是几十年来积累下来的死代码、hack 和变通方案？这两者非常难以区分。

paragraphs.en: I think of this as an example of a more general problem of dealing with legacy systems, which is… I think it's common for people to not understand why a legacy system does what it does. Like, a lot of the reason, it's not that the system was designed perfectly. It's that all sorts of things were wrong and people worked around the wrongness, and then you don't understand which parts of the system are reflecting good design decisions and which parts are reflecting hacks that are covering up for earlier bad design decisions. The only way to really understand all that is basically through archaeology.
paragraphs.zh: 我觉得这是处理遗留系统这个更普遍问题的一个典型案例。我认为，人们不理解一个遗留系统为什么要那样做，是很常见的现象。很多时候，原因并不是系统设计得很完美，而是各种各样的地方出了问题，然后人们针对这些问题打了补丁，最终你根本搞不清楚系统的哪些部分反映的是良好的设计决策，哪些部分反映的是为了掩盖早期糟糕决策而打的 hack。真正搞懂这一切，基本上只有通过考古才能做到。

paragraphs.en: Archaeology is a very good analogy. I felt that I was doing archaeology five years ago. It was just like: "Why is this variable here? I don't know. Maybe it's important, maybe it's not. If I remove it, maybe GHC will crash. Let me not do it."
paragraphs.zh: 考古是个很好的比喻。我五年前确实有种在做考古的感觉。就像：「这个变量为什么在这里？我也不知道。也许重要，也许不重要。如果我把它删了，GHC 可能会崩溃。算了还是不动它吧。」

paragraphs.en: It sounds like you solved the first 90% of the problem and now you have to solve the next 90% and then the next 90% after that. The problem doesn't go away. So you've written papers on build systems. What was that work about?
paragraphs.zh: 听起来你解决了问题的前 90%，然后还有下一个 90%，再然后还有下下个 90%。问题永远不会消失。那么你写了关于构建系统的论文，那些工作是关于什么的？

paragraphs.en: Yeah, sure. The first paper was about the limitations that we encountered in Make and why we decided to move from Make to Shake. This paper was about classifying build systems by a series of axes: how they determine what needs to be rebuilt, in other words how they track dependencies; how they schedule the build, in other words how they figure out what to execute in what order; and whether their scheduling is static or dynamic.
paragraphs.zh: 当然。第一篇论文是关于我们在 Make 中遇到的局限性，以及我们为什么决定从 Make 迁移到 Shake。这篇论文是通过一系列维度来对构建系统进行分类：它们如何确定什么需要重新构建，也就是如何追踪依赖；它们如何调度构建，也就是如何确定以什么顺序执行什么；以及它们的调度是静态的还是动态的。

paragraphs.en: Can you give us an example of a combination that doesn't, or at least at the time didn't exist?
paragraphs.zh: 你能举个例子，说明一种不存在的——或者至少在当时不存在的——组合吗？

paragraphs.en: So one such new combination is Cloud Shake. This combination comes from combining Shake, the build system, with Bazel, the build system developed by Google. And the idea is basically that you store your build outputs not locally, but in some cloud storage or a remote cache. And then when you need a particular build artifact, you first look it up in the cache and if it's there, you don't recompute it, you just use the cached version.
paragraphs.zh: 有一个这样的新组合叫做 Cloud Shake。这个组合来自于将 Shake 构建系统与 Google 开发的 Bazel 构建系统相结合。基本思路是：你不把构建产出物存储在本地，而是存储在某种云存储或远程缓存里。然后当你需要某个构建产物时，先在缓存里查找，如果找到了就不重新计算，直接使用缓存版本。

paragraphs.en: So what you're essentially in this case depending on is having some big lookup table in the sky, which maps build inputs to build outputs. And the advantage is that if lots of people are building the same thing, they don't all need to do the work independently. Only one person or one machine has to compute it and then everybody else gets to benefit from that.
paragraphs.zh: 所以在这种情况下，你本质上是依赖于云端的一张大查找表，它把构建输入映射到构建输出。其优势在于：如果很多人在构建同样的东西，他们不需要各自独立完成这项工作。只需要一个人或一台机器计算出来，然后其他所有人都可以从中受益。

paragraphs.en: That's right. Of course it's much easier to say than do. In our model was just the combination of the two approaches. But implementing this in a real build system is non-trivial because the build outputs can be very large and the lookups can be expensive, and various other practical considerations. It's a nice idea in theory and then it's a very hard engineering problem in practice.
paragraphs.zh: 对。当然，说起来比做起来容易多了。在我们的模型里，只是两种方式的结合。但在真实的构建系统中实现这一点并不简单，因为构建产出物可能非常大，查找也可能很耗费资源，还有各种其他实际考量。这在理论上是个好主意，在实践中却是个非常难的工程问题。

paragraphs.en: One thing that strikes me about the world of build systems is just how many of them there are. Not only are there loads of them, but there seem to be loads of them where none of them really want to talk to each other or coordinate well. So everything from Make to Bazel to Gradle to Cargo to Dune, they're all build systems and they all kind of do their own thing. What's going on there?
paragraphs.zh: 构建系统领域让我印象深刻的一点是，它们的数量实在太多了。不仅数量多，而且彼此之间似乎都不太愿意沟通或配合。Make、Bazel、Gradle、Cargo、Dune，它们都是构建系统，但都各自为政。这是怎么回事？

paragraphs.en: I guess this all comes from the fact that different communities have different infrastructures, have different needs. Different programming languages feel really naturally to build in different ways. So naturally, each community would build their own build system.
paragraphs.zh: 我觉得这都源于这样一个事实：不同的社区有不同的基础设施、不同的需求。不同的编程语言在构建方式上会自然地产生不同的风格。所以很自然地，每个社区都会建立自己的构建系统。

paragraphs.en: I agree with that but on some level, the fact that programming language is a thing that fractures the community is a little bit ironic, right? Because the whole point of build systems is to produce software artifacts and in principle, you should be able to have a build system that doesn't care what language it's in. So it's a little funny that the tool that's supposed to compose software together is itself not very composable.
paragraphs.zh: 我同意，但从某种层面来说，编程语言导致了社区的分裂，这有点讽刺，不是吗？因为构建系统存在的意义就是生成软件制品，原则上应该有一个不在乎你用什么语言的构建系统。所以这个本应把软件组合在一起的工具，本身却不太具有可组合性，这有点好笑。

paragraphs.en: Some build systems were designed with that in mind. It's pretty common to come across projects that use Bazel or CMake, where you can build things in multiple languages using a single build system. And in fact, there's actually a project called Pants, which builds on top of Bazel and it unifies build and test in a single interface that spans multiple programming languages.
paragraphs.zh: 有些构建系统在设计时就考虑到了这一点。遇到使用 Bazel 或 CMake 的项目挺常见的，这类系统可以用一个构建系统来构建多种语言的代码。事实上，还有一个叫 Pants 的项目，它建立在 Bazel 之上，将构建和测试统一在一个跨多种编程语言的界面中。

paragraphs.en: I feel like on the face of it, it might seem like using one of these language-agnostic build systems is clearly better, and yet at the same time, there seems to be this strong pull towards language-specific build systems. Why is that?
paragraphs.zh: 表面上看，使用这些语言无关的构建系统似乎明显更好，但与此同时，人们似乎又被语言特定的构建系统强烈地吸引着。这是为什么？

paragraphs.en: I've seen a few projects in this space where even trying to parse all the build descriptions in your project becomes really slow because the language-agnostic build system is just not great at efficiently building things for a given language. So there are definitely benefits from language-specific build systems, especially around tooling integration. You just know exactly what kind of tools you're dealing with and you can provide more specialized support.
paragraphs.zh: 我见过这个领域里的一些项目，仅仅是解析项目中所有构建描述就变得非常慢，因为语言无关的构建系统在为某种特定语言高效构建方面表现并不好。所以语言特定的构建系统确实有其优势，特别是在工具集成方面。你清楚地知道自己在处理什么类型的工具，因而可以提供更专业的支持。

paragraphs.en: Now let's switch gears and talk about what you've been doing most recently since in the last year you've joined Jane Street. And Jane Street uses OCaml and so building software at Jane Street means building OCaml software. And so there are two main build systems that are currently in use at Jane Street. One is Dune, which is an open source OCaml build system. And the other is a proprietary system called Jenga. Can you tell us a bit about each of them?
paragraphs.zh: 现在让我们换个话题，聊聊你最近在做什么——过去一年你加入了 Jane Street。Jane Street 使用 OCaml，所以在 Jane Street 构建软件意味着构建 OCaml 软件。Jane Street 目前主要使用两个构建系统，一个是 Dune，这是一个开源的 OCaml 构建系统；另一个是一个叫 Jenga 的内部系统。能跟我们分别介绍一下这两个系统吗？

paragraphs.en: The current build system is called Jenga and has been in production for a long time and it's a build system designed for building the Jane Street monorepo. And one very important property of a monorepo is that you have all the code in a single repository. So all the code depends on each other.
paragraphs.zh: 现在使用的构建系统叫 Jenga，已经在生产环境运行很长时间了，它是专门为构建 Jane Street 的 monorepo 而设计的。monorepo 有一个非常重要的特性：所有代码都在同一个代码库里，因此所有代码都互相依赖。

paragraphs.en: The joy of the monorepo is you get to build your code without ever having to say the words "constraint solving", which is great. Unlike a non-monorepo approach, where you have lots of independent projects, you have to figure out how to make all the versions compatible. And that, well, it's a solved problem but it's a deeply annoying problem.
paragraphs.zh: monorepo 的好处在于，你可以构建代码，而完全不需要说「约束求解」这个词，这真的很棒。不同于非 monorepo 的方式——你有很多独立的项目，就需要想办法让所有版本相互兼容。这个问题虽然有解决方案，但是个极其烦人的问题。

paragraphs.en: One way it is specialized to this use case is that the language that we use to describe the tasks that need to be performed is a limited functional language. So the rules for building code are expressed in this limited language and the important property of this language is that it's deterministic and has no side effects.
paragraphs.zh: 它针对这个使用场景的一个专门化之处在于：我们用来描述需要执行的任务的语言是一种受限的函数式语言。构建代码的规则就是用这种受限语言来表达的，这种语言的重要特性是它是确定性的，且没有副作用。

paragraphs.en: I want to talk more about Dune and in particular, the kind of shocking and embarrassing question of why a company that builds a build system would use an external build system. Can you say something about why both of these build systems are in use?
paragraphs.zh: 我想多聊聊 Dune，尤其是一个有点令人震惊和尴尬的问题：一家自己构建了构建系统的公司，为什么还要使用外部的构建系统？能说说为什么这两个构建系统都在使用吗？

paragraphs.en: In Jenga, we have two different levels at which things can be described. At the front-end, developers actually write rules in a subset of OCaml, called the rule description language. And this subset of OCaml is very limited, which makes it easy to make claims about it. Specifically, the claims we would like to make about it are that it's deterministic and has no I/O.
paragraphs.zh: 在 Jenga 中，我们有两个不同的描述层次。在前端，开发者实际上是用 OCaml 的一个子集来编写规则，这个子集叫做规则描述语言。这个 OCaml 的子集非常受限，这使得我们很容易对其做出一些保证。具体来说，我们希望能保证：它是确定性的，且没有 I/O 操作。

paragraphs.en: The basic structure here where you have two languages that you use for constructing build rules is actually not that unusual.
paragraphs.zh: 这种用两种语言来构建构建规则的基本结构，其实并不少见。

paragraphs.en: Yeah. It is pretty limited, and you want it to be limited. Like, what are the reasons why you want it to be limited? One is that a pure deterministic language is easier to reason about and, for example, to test. Another reason is that a limited language will have better tooling. For example, you might want to analyze the build rules statically, and for that a limited language is better.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，它非常受限，而且你就是希望它受限。那么为什么你希望它受限呢？一个原因是，纯粹的确定性语言更容易推理，比如也更容易测试。另一个原因是受限的语言会有更好的工具支持。比如，你可能想对构建规则做静态分析，受限的语言在这方面更有优势。

paragraphs.en: It's maybe not obvious, but determinism is really important for a bunch of the other things we talked about, including remote caching. If the same build step can produce different outputs on different machines, then you clearly can't use any of the outputs interchangeably. So determinism is kind of a fundamental requirement for getting some of the really nice properties.
paragraphs.zh: 这也许不是很显然，但确定性对于我们谈到的很多其他特性来说非常重要，包括远程缓存。如果同一个构建步骤在不同的机器上可以产生不同的输出，那你显然就无法互换使用这些输出了。所以确定性是获得那些真正好的特性的一个基本前提。

paragraphs.en: Yeah. Right now I'm actually working on moving Jenga along one of the axes in the paper. I'm working on adding remote caching, or what we call build farms, to Jenga. So currently, Jenga is a local build system in the sense that it stores all the build outputs locally on the developer's machine. And what I'm trying to do is to add an ability to also store the build outputs in some remote storage so that the build outputs can be shared across the machines.
paragraphs.zh: 是的。我现在其实正在推动 Jenga 沿着论文中的某个维度演进。我正在给 Jenga 添加远程缓存功能，也就是我们所说的构建农场。目前，Jenga 是一个本地构建系统，意思是它把所有构建产出物都存储在开发者本地机器上。我正在尝试添加一种能力，使构建产出物也可以存储在某个远程存储里，从而可以在多台机器之间共享。

paragraphs.en: How about in terms of the dynamic/static axis?
paragraphs.zh: 那在动态/静态这个维度上呢？

paragraphs.en: So Jenga supports dynamic dependencies and it uses a suspending scheduler as well. So basically it is on the dynamic side already. There are some interesting details about exactly how that works in the specific context of Jane Street but it's probably too much detail to go into.
paragraphs.zh: Jenga 支持动态依赖，它也使用了一个挂起式调度器。所以它基本上已经在动态这一侧了。在 Jane Street 的具体场景下，这具体是怎么工作的，有一些有趣的细节，但可能展开讲太多了。

paragraphs.en: It very much looks a lot like Shake does pre-cloud builds.
paragraphs.zh: 它看起来非常像云构建之前的 Shake。

paragraphs.en: That's right.
paragraphs.zh: 对，没错。

paragraphs.en: That sounds like a nice build system to have. Why does Jane Street have two build systems?
paragraphs.zh: 听起来是个很不错的构建系统。那 Jane Street 为什么会有两个构建系统呢？

paragraphs.en: In some sense this is an accident. OCaml ecosystem was using many different tools to build their projects. In fact, before the existence of Dune, there were like five or six tools for building OCaml code. And the idea behind Dune was to unify all of this into a single build system for OCaml. And it had one nice property in the beginning: it was very fast. It had a fast startup time.
paragraphs.zh: 某种意义上说，这是个意外。OCaml 生态系统使用了很多不同的工具来构建项目。事实上，在 Dune 出现之前，大概有五六种构建 OCaml 代码的工具。Dune 背后的想法就是把所有这些统一成一个 OCaml 的构建系统。它在一开始有一个很好的特性：非常快，启动时间很短。

paragraphs.en: It was fast when you wanted to build your project from scratch, but if you wanted an efficient incremental build, the story was different. Dune didn't really have a good incremental build story initially.
paragraphs.zh: 从头构建项目时它很快，但如果你需要高效的增量构建，情况就不一样了。Dune 最初并没有很好的增量构建支持。

paragraphs.en: Yeah, that's right.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，没错。

paragraphs.en: But it was a hell of a lot faster. A lot of work was put into the efficiency of parallel builds. Another thing that's great about Dune is that they've done a lot of work on making the user experience of the build system great. So for example, they've done a lot of work on diagnostic messages.
paragraphs.zh: 但它确实快得多。在并行构建效率上投入了大量工作。Dune 另一个出色的地方是，他们在提升构建系统的用户体验上做了很多工作，比如在错误诊断信息方面下了很大功夫。

paragraphs.en: Yeah. As this build system got more and more popular, Jérémie started to improve it bit by bit. For example, the incremental build that he added was initially just based on file watching and it was still doing too much work in terms of recompilation. But over time he added more and more features.
paragraphs.zh: 是的。随着这个构建系统越来越流行，Jérémie 开始一点一点地改进它。比如，他最初添加的增量构建只是基于文件监控，在重新编译方面仍然做了太多不必要的工作。但随着时间推移，他添加了越来越多的功能。

paragraphs.en: Highlighting the point that migration is difficult. I think it's getting close to being true that Dune can do everything that Jenga can do, but it's been kind of a slow process.
paragraphs.zh: 这再次说明迁移是很困难的。我觉得 Dune 能做 Jenga 能做的一切这件事正在慢慢成真，但这是个相当缓慢的过程。

paragraphs.en: Yeah, absolutely. I think maybe for any two build systems, you just can never compare them. No build system is strictly better than another. So even if Dune is to some extent better than Jenga in some ways, there will be other ways where it's not as good.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，完全同意。我觉得对于任意两个构建系统，你真的很难直接比较它们。没有哪个构建系统在所有方面都严格优于另一个。所以即使 Dune 在某些方面在某种程度上比 Jenga 好，在其他方面也会有它不如 Jenga 的地方。

paragraphs.en: One of the hard parts about doing a migration like this, you're trying to change out the wheels on a moving car. Which is to say as Dune gets built up, Jenga is also getting developed. And so you're sort of chasing a moving target. Is that a fair description?
paragraphs.zh: 做这种迁移，难点之一是你在试图给一辆行驶中的汽车换轮子。也就是说，Dune 在不断发展的同时，Jenga 也在持续开发。所以你某种程度上是在追一个移动的目标。这个描述准确吗？

paragraphs.en: Whenever we implement a new feature in Jenga, we know that we will actually have to implement something equivalent in Dune as well. So there's definitely a feeling that you're doing double work, and that's unfortunate.
paragraphs.zh: 每当我们在 Jenga 里实现一个新功能，就知道我们在 Dune 里也必须实现等价的功能。所以确实有一种在做双重工作的感觉，这很令人遗憾。

paragraphs.en: Is there anything that you guys are doing to try and reduce the amount of dual work going forward?
paragraphs.zh: 你们有没有在做什么，来减少未来这种重复工作的量？

paragraphs.en: Right now at least we would like to stage some of this work in the sense that we are splitting Dune into a library and a frontend. So the idea is to split Dune's internals into a library layer, which is the core of the build engine, and a front-end layer, which is the user-facing part.
paragraphs.zh: 至少现在，我们希望分阶段推进这项工作，方式是把 Dune 拆分成一个库和一个前端。思路是把 Dune 的内部分为两层：一个库层，也就是构建引擎的核心；以及一个前端层，也就是面向用户的部分。

paragraphs.en: Got it. I guess one of the key pieces there is getting it so that you don't have to maintain two sets of the really hairy build engine semantics. You just have one set and then you have two different front ends on top of that.
paragraphs.zh: 明白了。我猜其中一个关键点就是，让你不必维护两套非常复杂的构建引擎语义。你只需要维护一套，然后在此之上有两个不同的前端。

paragraphs.en: This is exactly what we are trying to do. We would like to start sharing some code as soon as possible. And one candidate is the build engine, where we don't want to maintain both the Jenga and Dune build engines. We would like to basically just use one of them. And the plan currently is to actually port Jenga to use the Dune build engine under the hood.
paragraphs.zh: 这正是我们正在尝试做的。我们希望尽快开始共享一些代码。其中一个候选是构建引擎，我们不想同时维护 Jenga 和 Dune 两个构建引擎。我们希望基本上只使用其中一个。目前的计划是将 Jenga 移植为在底层使用 Dune 的构建引擎。

paragraphs.en: At least the two systems are in the same language. That simplifies it a little bit.
paragraphs.zh: 至少这两个系统用的是同一种语言，这多少简化了一些。

paragraphs.en: That's right.
paragraphs.zh: 对，确实。

paragraphs.en: So when you've talked about the ways in which build systems have evolved over time, a lot of the points you made were about better integration with new features or new services. And I'd love to hear a bit more about what that looks like in the future. What do you think is going to change about build systems over the next 10 or 20 years?
paragraphs.zh: 当你谈到构建系统随时间演进的方式时，你提到的很多点都是关于与新功能或新服务更好地集成。我想多了解一下未来会是什么样子。你认为在未来 10 到 20 年里，构建系统会发生什么变化？

paragraphs.en: I'll probably go back to that example of everyday tasks that a software developer needs to do. As you said, build systems are increasingly being used to automate all of these tasks: they run tests, they build documentation, they do static analysis. And one thing that I would like to see is better integration of build systems with IDEs and code editors. And I already see this happening, like Language Server Protocol was a big step in this direction.
paragraphs.zh: 我可能还是会回到软件开发者日常需要完成的任务这个例子。就像你说的，构建系统越来越多地被用来自动化所有这些任务：运行测试、构建文档、做静态分析。有一件事是我希望看到的，那就是构建系统与 IDE 和代码编辑器更好地集成。我已经看到这种趋势了，比如语言服务协议（Language Server Protocol）就是这个方向上的一大步。

paragraphs.en: I feel like some of those integrations you described are pretty different from others. So the integration with testing is great, but testing and building have always been closely linked. The integration with IDEs I think is actually a really big deal, and it's sort of a bigger qualitative shift. Would you agree that integration with IDEs is sort of a qualitative shift in what build systems are doing?
paragraphs.zh: 我感觉你描述的那些集成有些性质上有很大差异。测试集成固然很好，但测试和构建一直以来都是紧密相连的。而与 IDE 的集成，我认为其实是件很大的事，是一种更大的质的转变。你同意与 IDE 的集成是构建系统所做事情的一种质的转变吗？

paragraphs.en: That's right.
paragraphs.zh: 对，没错。

paragraphs.en: So there's another kind of integration, which I haven't actually seen happen in a general purpose build system, which is a much deeper integration with the compiler itself. So when the build system starts the build, it essentially creates a new compiler process for each build rule and this creates a lot of overhead. What I would like to see in the future is a much closer integration with the compiler so that instead of spawning new compiler processes, the build system and the compiler would be actually merged or tightly coupled, so you don't pay this overhead.
paragraphs.zh: 还有另一种集成，我在通用构建系统里还没有真正见过，那就是与编译器本身更深层次的集成。目前，当构建系统开始构建时，它本质上是为每条构建规则创建一个新的编译器进程，这会带来大量开销。我希望未来能看到与编译器更紧密的集成：不再是启动新的编译器进程，而是构建系统和编译器真正地融合或紧密耦合，这样就不需要承担这些开销了。

paragraphs.en: In some sense, we start moving from automating tasks of developers to automating tasks of a compiler, meaning we're sort of treating the compiler as something that the build system can reach inside and improve the performance of.
paragraphs.zh: 从某种意义上说，我们开始从自动化开发者的任务，转向自动化编译器的任务，也就是说，我们某种程度上把编译器视为构建系统可以深入介入并提升其性能的对象。

paragraphs.en: Yeah. I also think one of the challenges there is that as you try and drive the granularity of the operations that you parallelize and memoize, you push more and more into the internals of the compiler. And that's a direction in which some languages have gone, most notably Rust and its compilation model.
paragraphs.zh: 是的。我还认为其中一个挑战在于，当你试图细化并行化和记忆化操作的粒度时，你就越来越深入到编译器内部了。这是某些语言已经走的方向，最典型的是 Rust 及其编译模型。

paragraphs.en: Yeah. For integrating with the compiler, I think at some point we will reach the point where the build system and the compiler will be so tightly integrated that you won't really be able to distinguish between them. It'll just be one system that does everything.
paragraphs.zh: 是的。对于与编译器的集成，我认为在某个时刻，构建系统和编译器的集成会紧密到你根本无法区分它们的程度。它就会是一个做所有事情的统一系统。

paragraphs.en: Yeah. Although the painful part about that is I worry, it will drive things even more to be language-specific. Like Rust can do a good job of fusing the build system and the compiler, but then it's specific to Rust. And that would kind of suck, I think, if the world further fragmentized along these lines.
paragraphs.zh: 是的。不过这里令人担忧的地方是，我担心这会让一切变得更加语言特定。比如 Rust 可以很好地融合构建系统和编译器，但那就是 Rust 特定的了。如果世界沿着这条线进一步碎片化，我觉得会挺糟糕的。

paragraphs.en: Yeah. So Excel superficially might look very different, but it's also an incremental computation engine in some sense. And in fact, in our paper there's a section dedicated to Excel, which is actually I think one of the most used incremental computation systems out there. And what Excel is doing, well it's doing something fairly similar to what a build system does in the sense that you have some cells and the cells depend on other cells, and when you change one cell, you want to make sure that all the downstream cells that depend on it are recomputed.
paragraphs.zh: 是的。Excel 表面上看起来可能非常不同，但从某种意义上说，它也是一个增量计算引擎。事实上，在我们的论文里专门有一节是关于 Excel 的，我认为它实际上是目前使用最广泛的增量计算系统之一。Excel 所做的事情，与构建系统所做的事情相当类似：你有一些单元格，单元格依赖于其他单元格，当你改变一个单元格时，你希望确保所有依赖它的下游单元格都被重新计算。

paragraphs.en: I should say that this for sure includes us. We have a ton of massive spreadsheets and recomputing everything on every update of a cell would just be way too slow.
paragraphs.zh: 我要说这肯定也包括我们。我们有大量的大型电子表格，每次更新一个单元格就重新计算所有内容会慢得离谱。

paragraphs.en: So I guess the main difference is that like Excel and other incremental computational systems that don't target software development have to deal with live values. You don't want to persist the value to disk, you want to keep it in memory. So in that sense, a build system is kind of a batch computation that stores all of its outputs to disk. And an incremental computational system like Excel operates in memory.
paragraphs.zh: 所以我觉得主要的区别在于：像 Excel 这样不面向软件开发的增量计算系统，需要处理的是活的值。你不想把值持久化到磁盘，你想把它保留在内存里。从这个意义上说，构建系统是一种把所有输出存储到磁盘的批处理计算，而像 Excel 这样的增量计算系统则在内存中操作。

paragraphs.en: So one of the interesting things about including Excel in that paper is I feel like it opens a certain door, which is recognizing that there's this much broader category of computation that build systems are a special case of. And it makes you wonder whether there are yet other kinds of computation which could benefit from the techniques that build systems bring: incrementality, parallelism, remote caching and so on.
paragraphs.zh: 在论文中包含 Excel 这件事有一个有趣之处：我觉得它打开了一扇门，让我们认识到存在一个更宽泛的计算类别，而构建系统只是其中的一个特例。这让人不禁想到，是否还有其他类型的计算可以从构建系统带来的技术中受益：增量计算、并行性、远程缓存等等。

paragraphs.en: I think it's easy to say that conceptually all these things are the same as we do in our paper, but practically they are very different. The incremental computation for scientific computing is completely different from what you have in a build system, and that's because the computations involved are very different.
paragraphs.zh: 我认为在概念上说这些东西都是一样的很容易，就像我们在论文里做的那样，但在实践中它们非常不同。科学计算中的增量计算与构建系统中的完全不同，这是因为所涉及的计算本质上就是不同的。

paragraphs.en: Right. And even when we do extend beyond a single machine in the context of ordinary build systems, we still have, typically, strong assumptions about the whole graph being structured: you have source files which are your inputs, and you have build outputs which are your final artifacts, and the graph is a DAG. Whereas I imagine in the context of scientific computing, the computation is much more irregular in structure.
paragraphs.zh: 对。即使在普通构建系统的场景下扩展到多台机器，我们通常对整个图的结构仍然有很强的假设：你有作为输入的源文件，有作为最终产物的构建输出，整个图是一个有向无环图（DAG）。而在科学计算的场景中，我想计算的结构要不规则得多。

paragraphs.en: Yes, one other example of a difference that shows up in scientific computations is that those computations can run for very long time. So your computation can literally be running for days or even months. And that's very different from a build system perspective where your goal is to make things fast.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，科学计算中另一个体现差异的例子是，那些计算可以运行非常长的时间。你的计算可以字面意义上运行几天甚至几个月。这与构建系统的视角非常不同，因为构建系统的目标是让事情快起来。

paragraphs.en: It's funny. It reminds me of the way we used to write programs. My father was a computer scientist and would tell me about the time when programs were submitted in the form of punch card decks and you would submit a program and come back to look at it the next day, and sometimes it would be finished and sometimes there would be an error and you would go through the output looking for where the error was. And that kind of sounds like what you're describing with scientific computing, this thing where you do your computation, you come back later and either you have an error or you have a result.
paragraphs.zh: 这挺有意思的。它让我想起了我们以前写程序的方式。我父亲是个计算机科学家，他会跟我讲那个年代提交打孔卡片程序的事：你提交一个程序，第二天回来看，有时候它跑完了，有时候会有个错误，然后你就翻输出找错误在哪。这听起来很像你描述的科学计算——你做完计算，过一段时间回来，要么有个错误，要么有个结果。

paragraphs.en: Scientific computation is like on the one side of the range where we have the longest running jobs and the most specialized hardware: GPUs and so on. On the other side of the spectrum is something like Excel, which is trying to just be totally interactive and has to finish within milliseconds. And build systems are somewhere in between.
paragraphs.zh: 科学计算处于这个范围的一端，那里有运行时间最长的任务和最专用的硬件：GPU 等等。另一端是像 Excel 这样的东西，它追求完全的交互性，必须在毫秒内完成。而构建系统则处于两者之间。

paragraphs.en: That's right. Build systems, you normally get down to some fairly coarse grained graph representation where each node in the graph corresponds to compiling some set of files. Whereas in scientific computing, you might want the granularity to be much finer, like individual functions. And the intermediate data is just much bigger.
paragraphs.zh: 对。构建系统通常会用到相当粗粒度的图表示，图中每个节点对应编译某组文件。而在科学计算中，你可能希望粒度细得多，比如精细到单个函数级别。而且中间数据的体量也大得多。

paragraphs.en: Yeah. Thanks. That was a lot of fun, I agree, and I'll be tuning into the other podcasts that you have. Thank you so much, Ron.
paragraphs.zh: 好的，谢谢。这真的很有趣，我同意，我会继续收听你的其他播客的。非常感谢你，Ron。

paragraphs.en: You can find links to a bunch of the things we talked about, including Andrey's papers on build systems at signalsandthreads.com. If you have thoughts on this episode or suggestions for future ones, write to us at signalsandthreads@janestreet.com. And if you enjoyed the show, please take a moment to rate and review us. It makes a real difference in helping us find new listeners. Thanks for listening.
paragraphs.zh: 你可以在 signalsandthreads.com 找到我们今天谈到的很多内容的链接，包括 Andrey 关于构建系统的论文。如果你对这期节目有什么想法，或者对未来节目有什么建议，请写信给我们：signalsandthreads@janestreet.com。如果你喜欢这个节目，请花一点时间给我们评分和评论，这对我们找到新听众真的很有帮助。感谢收听。
